Trial Initiation: Protocol, ICF, and IB are authored in Vault RIM (as regulatory documents) and simultaneously filed in Vault eTMF Zone 2. The same document record in Vault, with the same document ID, version, and audit trail, serves both purposes — single source of truth, zero duplication.
2
Protocol Amendments: When a protocol amendment is authored in Vault RIM (driven by a safety finding or regulatory request), the submission workflow publishes it to FDA as an IND amendment; simultaneously, the new protocol version is routed to Vault eTMF Zone 2 and all affected site ICFs are flagged for update. All sites receive a task notification via Vault to file the updated documents at the site level (Zone 5).
3
Safety Reporting (SUSAR): Pharmacovigilance identifies a SUSAR in the safety database; the SUSAR report is authored, reviewed through a Vault Quality workflow, and transmitted to FDA as an expedited IND safety report via RIM. Simultaneously, the updated IB (if the SUSAR meets the threshold) is version-bumped in Vault RIM, pushed to eTMF Zone 2, and distributed to all active sites for ICF revision.
4
Monitoring Reports & Protocol Deviations: Clinical Research Associates (CRAs) file monitoring visit reports and protocol deviation records directly into Vault eTMF Zone 5 after each site visit. These are automatically flagged for Clinical Quality Assurance review. Significant deviations that require regulatory reporting are escalated via a Vault workflow to Regulatory Affairs, who assesses whether an IND amendment or safety report is needed — triggering a new submission workflow in RIM.
5
Clinical Study Report (CSR) Lock: When a trial completes and the CSR is finalised, it is filed in Vault eTMF Zone 2 (closing out the trial-level TMF) and simultaneously linked in Vault RIM as a source document for Module 5 of the NDA. The same document, in the same Vault, flows from the clinical record into the submission record without re-upload or format conversion.
6
NDA Assembly & Publishing: The Submissions module in RIM aggregates all linked source documents (CSRs from eTMF, quality documents from Vault QMS, manufacturing data from Vault QualityDocs) and assembles the CTD. Module 2 summaries and the integrated summaries of efficacy/safety are authored directly in Vault RIM by regulatory writers. The built-in eCTD publisher compiles the validated submission package for ESG delivery to FDA.
7
FDA Review & Information Requests: FDA Information Requests (IRs) arrive as HA Correspondence records in Vault RIM; they are triaged, assigned, and tracked. Response documents are authored in Vault, reviewed through the submission workflow, and published as a sequence 0001 Amendment within the NDA lifecycle. Every IR and response becomes part of the permanent regulatory record linked to the NDA in RIM.
8
Approval → Registration → Commercial Launch: Upon receipt of the FDA Action Letter (approval), Vault RIM auto-creates a Registration record for the approved product in the US market; the approved label is filed as the current labelling version; post-approval commitments and REMS obligations (if any) are entered as Regulatory Commitments with due dates. This registration record immediately becomes the basis for global variation submissions — EU, Japan, and other markets receive variation submissions built from the same approved US dossier, adapted to local requirements through the Labelling module.
💡 Why Veeva Vault's Single-Platform Architecture Matters for Regulatory
The critical differentiator of the Veeva approach is that eTMF and RIM are not separate databases connected by integrations — they are modules of a single Vault platform sharing the same document repository, the same user management, the same audit trail infrastructure, and the same API. A protocol document in Zone 2 of eTMF is the same object as the source document linked in the NDA Module 5 CTD in RIM — the same document ID, the same metadata, the same version history. This matters enormously for inspection readiness: when an FDA inspector asks "show me every version of Protocol Amendment 3 and every submission that references it," Vault answers that query across both the trial record and the regulatory record in a single search, with a complete audit trail showing who filed, approved, and referenced each version. The alternative — paper TMFs, SharePoint folders, and disconnected submission management tools — makes this question take days to answer. In Vault, it takes seconds.
←Ch 1 · The Big Picture
◈
Ch 3 · Commercial Affairs→
3
Chapter Three

Commercial Affairs — The Business of Getting Medicine to Patients

Commercial Affairs is the engine that drives revenue. Without it, no pharmaceutical company can fund the next generation of research. It encompasses everything from the big strategic plans that set direction for years, down to the single sales representative who shakes a doctor's hand in a clinic hallway.

🍕 Think of it this way — The Pizza Shop Analogy
Imagine a pizza shop that invented a new, medically-superior pizza that actually lowers blood pressure. The scientists made it. But now someone has to:

• Tell all the nutritionists (doctors) about it — Sales Force
• Figure out which neighbourhoods need it most — Market Research
• Create flyers and menus that are 100% accurate — Content & MLR
• Negotiate with health insurance to cover part of the cost — Market Access
• Give free samples to nutritionists so they can try it — Sample Management
• Watch what the competing pizza shops are doing — Competitive Intelligence

Commercial Affairs does all of that — but for life-saving medicines, with extremely strict rules about what you can say and how you can behave.

◈ 📊 Commercial Affairs — End-to-End Process Flow
2. Commercial Affairs — End-to-End Process Flow Commercial
8 interconnected processes from brand strategy through competitive intelligence — with key inputs and outputs at each stage
>3.1 Strategic Brand Planning — The Master Blueprint

Every product in a pharmaceutical company has a Brand Team — a cross-functional group of people dedicated to that specific drug. The Brand Manager (sometimes called Product Manager) is like the CEO of that single product. They own its strategy, its budget, and its performance.

Every year, the Brand Team creates an Annual Brand Plan. This is the master blueprint for everything that will happen with the product in the next 12 months. Creating it takes months of analysis, debate, and refinement.

The Brand Planning Process — Step by Step

Situation Analysis
→
Strategic Imperatives
→
Goals & Targets
→
Tactical Plan
→
Budget
→
Execute
→
QBR Review
→
Course Correct

Step 1 — Situation Analysis: The team asks: Where are we today? They look at sales data, market share, competitor performance, payer coverage levels, doctor awareness, patient outcomes, and economic trends. It's like a doctor taking a patient's vitals before diagnosing anything.

Step 2 — Strategic Imperatives: From the analysis, they identify the 3–5 most important battles to win in the coming year. For example: "We need to grow our market share in community oncology centres" or "We need to improve insurance coverage from Tier 3 to Tier 2 with the three largest health plans."

Step 3 — Goals & Targets: They set specific, measurable goals — how many new prescriptions per week? What market share percentage? How many doctors to reach?

Step 4 — Tactical Plan: Every strategy gets translated into specific activities — campaigns, events, digital outreach, sales calls, payer meetings, patient programs.

Step 5 — Budget: Money is allocated across all tactics. A typical commercial launch for a specialty drug might spend $50–200 million in Year 1.

Steps 6–8 — Execute, Review, and Adjust: Every quarter, the team holds a Quarterly Business Review (QBR) — an honest assessment of what's working and what isn't. If a region is underperforming, resources shift. If a competitor makes an unexpected move, the strategy adapts.

◈ 📊 Brand Planning Process — Visual Flow
4. Brand Planning Cycle & MLR Review Process Commercial Compliance
Annual brand planning cycle (left) and the MLR content review decision tree (right)
>3.2 Market Research & Customer Insights — Listening Before Talking

You cannot sell a medicine effectively if you don't understand the people who prescribe it, the patients who take it, or the system that pays for it. Market Research is the intelligence function that answers those questions.

🔍 Think of it this way — The Detective Analogy
A market researcher is like a detective. They don't guess — they gather evidence. They interview witnesses (doctors and patients), study historical records (prescription data), and build a complete picture of reality before any strategy is formed. Without this work, the brand team would be making expensive decisions based on gut feeling rather than fact.

Primary Research involves going directly to the source: conducting surveys with doctors, running focus groups with patients, observing how a doctor actually moves through their day in a clinic (called ethnographic research). The goal is to understand real behaviours and real needs — not what people think they should say, but what they actually do.

Secondary Research uses existing data. The most important type is prescription data — syndicated datasets (from companies like IQVIA or Symphony Health) that show exactly which doctors are prescribing which drugs, how many prescriptions per week, for which types of patients. This data is the lifeblood of commercial operations.

Patient Journey Mapping traces the complete path a patient takes from the first symptom to treatment. A typical journey might look like this:

Symptom Onset
→
Primary Care Visit
→
Specialist Referral
→
Diagnosis
→
Treatment Decision
→
Prescription
→
Adherence
→
Outcome

At every step, there are barriers (why patients don't get treated) and opportunities (where the company can help). Market research identifies both.

3.3 Sales Force Effectiveness (SFE) & Field Operations — The Human Engine

The sales force is the most visible face of a pharmaceutical company. Territory Managers (the official title for what most people call "drug reps") visit doctors' offices, clinics, and hospitals to educate healthcare professionals about the company's medicines.

📌 Key Concept — What a Sales Rep Actually Does
A pharmaceutical sales representative does NOT sell drugs to patients. They cannot do that — only doctors can prescribe drugs. What they do is educate healthcare professionals (HCPs) — doctors, nurse practitioners, physician assistants — about the approved uses, benefits, and risks of the company's medicines. Every single thing they say must come from the FDA-approved label. They cannot exaggerate, cannot make promises about off-label uses, and cannot provide incentives that could be seen as bribes.

How the Sales Force is Organized and Deployed

Sales Force Sizing
→
Territory Design
→
Target List Creation
→
Call Planning
→
Field Execution
→
Performance Tracking
→
IC Payment

Sales Force Sizing: How many reps do you need? This is a math problem. The SFE team calculates how many doctors need to be reached, how often each doctor needs to be visited, how many visits one rep can make per day, and how large a territory one person can cover. The answer gives the optimal headcount.

Territory Design: Territories are drawn so that each rep has roughly equal workload and potential. When reps join or leave, or when a product gets a new indication, territories are realigned. This is a geospatial analysis challenge that requires sophisticated software.

Target List Creation: Not all doctors are equal. A small percentage of doctors prescribe the vast majority of a drug's volume. The targeting team identifies which doctors are the highest-priority — Tier A (top prescribers, highest potential), Tier B (medium), and Tier C (low). Tier A doctors get the most visits.

Incentive Compensation (IC): Sales reps are paid a base salary plus a variable bonus tied to performance — prescription growth in their territory, market share improvement, and other metrics. Designing IC plans is complex: they must motivate performance without creating incentives to promote off-label or to behave unethically.

3.4 Promotional Content & the MLR Review — Every Word Must Be True

Every brochure a rep leaves with a doctor, every email sent to a healthcare professional, every banner ad on a medical website, every patient pamphlet — every single piece of promotional material — must be approved by a committee before it can be used. This process is called MLR Review: Medical, Legal, Regulatory.

⚠️ Why MLR Is Non-Negotiable
In 2009, Pfizer paid $2.3 billion — the largest healthcare fraud settlement in history — partly for promoting drugs for uses not approved by the FDA (off-label promotion). In 2012, GlaxoSmithKline paid $3 billion for similar violations. The MLR process exists precisely to prevent these catastrophic failures. It is not red tape. It is a legal and ethical guardrail that protects patients, doctors, and the company itself.

The MLR Review Process — Step by Step

Content Brief
→
Agency Draft
→
Medical Review
→
Legal Review
→
Regulatory Review
→
Approved? ✓ / Revise ↺
→
Field Release

The Brand Team writes a content brief — what the piece is trying to communicate, who the audience is, what claims will be made, and what evidence supports them. An advertising or medical communications agency creates the actual piece — copy, design, references.

Then the three-reviewer committee examines it:

  • Medical Review: A Medical Director or Physician checks every claim for scientific accuracy. Is this claim supported by the data? Does the piece create a misleading impression even if individual statements are technically true? Are risks presented fairly alongside benefits?
  • Legal Review: A lawyer reviews for intellectual property issues, competitive claims that could create legal exposure, and liability language.
  • Regulatory Review: A Regulatory Affairs specialist checks compliance with FDA promotional guidelines — specifically 21 CFR Part 202 — and confirms that no claim goes beyond the approved label.

If any reviewer objects, the piece goes back to the agency for revision and re-review. This cycle can repeat multiple times. When all three approve, the piece is released. Approved pieces have a validity period — usually two years. Expired materials must be immediately pulled from use.

◈ 📊 MLR Review Workflow — Step by Step
>3.5 HCP Engagement & Relationship Management — Building Trust With Doctors

HCP stands for Healthcare Professional — doctors, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, pharmacists. Building productive relationships with them is the core of Commercial activities. But these relationships must be genuine, educational, and scrupulously compliant with regulations.

Multi-channel engagement means reaching HCPs through multiple touchpoints: in-person rep visits, phone calls, approved emails, digital platforms, speaker programs (educational dinners where a doctor presents clinical data to their peers), and medical congresses.

📌 The Sunshine Act — All Money Flows Are Public
The Physician Payments Sunshine Act (now called Open Payments) requires pharmaceutical companies to report every dollar of value they transfer to physicians or teaching hospitals. This includes meals provided during a sales call, speaker fees, consulting fees, travel, gifts, and education. All this data is published publicly on the CMS Open Payments website so patients can see what their doctors receive from pharmaceutical companies. Compliance teams reconcile every single payment.

◈ 📊 HCP Journey & Engagement Flow
9. HCP Engagement Journey Commercial Medical
How Commercial and Medical Affairs engage the same HCP at different stages — from unaware to advocate — while maintaining the firewall
>3.6 Market Access — Making Sure Patients Can Actually Afford It

A drug that works but that patients cannot afford is a failure. Market Access is the function that ensures patients can actually get the medicine through their health insurance — and at a price the company can sustain.

🏦 Think of it this way — The Insurance Analogy
Imagine you invent a revolutionary new type of hearing aid. But health insurance companies won't automatically cover it. You have to prove to their medical directors that it is clinically effective AND cost-effective — meaning it delivers better health outcomes without costing significantly more than alternatives. Only then will they add it to their list of covered products (the "formulary"). Market Access is the team that makes this case to every major insurer in the country.

Formulary Placement is the critical outcome. Every health plan maintains a formulary — a list of covered drugs and their cost to the patient. Tier 1 = cheapest for the patient (usually generics). Tier 2 = preferred brand drugs (moderate co-pay). Tier 3 = non-preferred brands (high co-pay). A drug stuck on Tier 3 will struggle to gain volume because patients face high out-of-pocket costs.

To secure Tier 2 placement, the company typically agrees to pay rebates — essentially discounts — to the health plan or pharmacy benefit manager (PBM). The negotiation of these rebates is one of the most commercially sensitive activities in the industry.

Prior Authorization (PA) is another barrier. Some health plans require the prescribing doctor to submit additional justification before a patient can get the drug covered. The Market Access team works to minimize PA requirements and helps HCPs navigate PA processes when they exist.

Patient Access Programs — co-pay cards (reduce what insured patients pay out of pocket) and patient assistance programs (free drug for uninsured patients) — are the safety net that ensures even financially vulnerable patients can access the medicine.

3.7 Sample Management — The Most Regulated Handshake in Business

When a sales representative leaves free drug samples at a doctor's office, this seemingly simple act is governed by one of the most rigorous compliance frameworks in the industry — the Prescription Drug Marketing Act (PDMA).

Every sample must be tracked from the manufacturer's warehouse to the doctor's sample closet. Every distribution requires the doctor's signature. The rep must maintain a physical inventory count that reconciles with the central system. The doctor must be credentialed — their license verified as active — before receiving samples. And the FDA can audit the entire chain of custody at any time.

Why such rigor? Drug samples have historically been a vector for fraud (stolen and sold on the black market) and misuse (doctors using samples for themselves or their families). PDMA exists to prevent these abuses.

3.8 Competitive Intelligence — Knowing the Battlefield

No pharmaceutical product exists in a vacuum. Competing drugs, competing therapeutic approaches, and competing companies are always present. Competitive Intelligence (CI) is the systematic, ethical process of monitoring what competitors are doing and translating that into strategic insight.

CI teams monitor competitor pipeline developments (which drugs are in Phase 3 that might compete with your product?), competitor label changes, competitor pricing and access moves, and field intelligence gathered from sales representatives who hear what doctors are saying about competing products. This information flows to brand strategy, medical affairs, and market access to inform defensive and offensive moves.

←Ch 2 · R&D — Where It Begins
◈
Ch 4 · Medical Affairs→
4
Chapter Four

Medical Affairs — The Scientific Conscience of the Company

While Commercial Affairs focuses on revenue, Medical Affairs focuses on scientific truth. It is the function that ensures a pharmaceutical company earns — and keeps — the trust of the medical community. Without that trust, even the best-marketed drug fails.

⚖️ Think of it this way — The Judge Analogy
If Commercial Affairs is like a lawyer arguing their client's case (the drug is great, prescribe it!), Medical Affairs is like the judge in the courtroom — independent, evidence-driven, and committed to the truth above all else. Medical Affairs cannot argue the drug is better than the evidence shows. They cannot hide unfavourable data. And when a doctor asks a complex scientific question, they answer it honestly — even if the honest answer is not commercially flattering.

◈ 📊 Medical Affairs — End-to-End Process Flow
3. Medical Affairs — End-to-End Process Flow Medical Affairs
6 core processes from scientific strategy through pharmacovigilance — scientific integrity maintained throughout
>4.1 Medical Strategy & Evidence Generation — What We Know, What We Need to Know

After the pivotal Phase 3 trial, thousands of clinical questions remain unanswered. The Phase 3 trial proves the drug works in carefully controlled conditions — but real-world medical practice is messier. Doctors need to know:

  • How does the drug perform in elderly patients? (Phase 3 often excludes people over 75.)
  • What happens when patients have kidney disease? Liver disease?
  • How does it compare to the drug that doctors already use?
  • What are the outcomes in real patients, not clinical trial participants?
  • Can it be used in combination with other common medications?

The Medical Gap Analysis is the systematic process of identifying these unanswered questions and prioritizing them. From this comes the Medical Plan — a 3-year strategic roadmap for generating the scientific evidence that doctors, payers, and guideline committees need.

Types of Evidence Generation Studies

Phase 3b Trials
Supplemental Controlled Trials

Additional randomized controlled trials exploring specific patient subgroups, dosing variations, or new endpoints not included in the pivotal study. These expand the scientific base without changing the core approval.

Phase 4 / Post-Marketing Studies
FDA-Mandated Surveillance

Often required by the FDA as a condition of approval. These long-term studies monitor safety in real-world populations over years — capturing rare side effects that only emerge at massive scale.

Real-World Evidence (RWE) Studies
Observational Research

Studies based on claims data, electronic health records, and patient registries that show how the drug performs outside the controlled environment of a clinical trial. Payers love RWE because it reflects actual clinical practice.

Investigator-Initiated Studies (IIS)
Independent Research

External researchers (usually academic physicians) propose independent studies. The company provides funding, drug supply, and sometimes data — but does NOT control the study. IIS-generated data is more credible to guideline committees because of its independence.

4.2 Medical Science Liaisons (MSLs) — The Scientific Ambassadors

MSLs are arguably the most important people in Medical Affairs. They are the scientific field force — experts with advanced degrees (PhDs, PharmDs, MDs) who work directly with the medical community.

📌 Key Concept — What Makes an MSL Different From a Sales Rep
A sales representative presents approved promotional content and is measured by prescription impact. An MSL has peer-level scientific conversations with Key Opinion Leaders (KOLs) — discussing data, sharing published research, responding to unsolicited medical questions, and collecting scientific insights. An MSL can discuss competitor data. An MSL can discuss data that is not commercially flattering. A sales rep cannot do any of these things. This is why the firewall between MSLs and Sales is so important.

Key Opinion Leaders (KOLs) are the most influential physicians in a therapeutic area. They publish landmark papers, chair guideline committees, present at major medical congresses, and teach the next generation of doctors. When a KOL changes their prescribing practice, hundreds of their peers often follow. MSLs identify, profile, and build scientific relationships with these individuals — not to convert them to prescribers (that is Commercial's job), but to engage them as scientific thought partners.

MSL Engagement Activities

KOL Identification & Profiling
→
Scientific Exchange Meetings
→
Advisory Board Facilitation
→
IIS Identification
→
Insight Capture
→
Congress Presence

Insight Capture is one of the MSL's most valuable contributions. In every scientific conversation, MSLs capture what KOLs are saying — what clinical questions they have, what unmet needs they see, what they think of the company's drug and its competitors, and what evidence they would need to change their practice. These insights flow back to Medical Strategy (to refine the evidence plan), Brand Strategy (to sharpen messaging), and Regulatory Affairs (to inform label discussions).

Advisory Boards are structured meetings where 8–15 KOLs are convened (and paid fairly at market rate) to provide scientific guidance on a specific question — often the design of a new clinical study, the interpretation of existing data, or the scientific landscape of the therapeutic area. Advisory boards generate the most concentrated scientific intelligence available to Medical Affairs.

4.3 Medical Information & Scientific Communications — Answering Every Question

Every day, healthcare professionals have questions about the company's drugs that go beyond what a sales representative can answer. A cardiologist wants to know what happens when the drug is taken alongside blood thinners. A pharmacist asks about the stability of the drug in a nasogastric tube. A patient's mother wants to know if her daughter can take it while breastfeeding.

The Medical Information (MI) Center — staffed by pharmacists (PharmDs), nurses, and physicians — answers all of these questions. Every response comes from reviewed, scientifically accurate materials called Standard Response Letters (SRLs) — pre-approved documents that answer common questions.

⚠️ The Off-Label Minefield
When a doctor asks about a use of the drug not covered by the FDA-approved label — called an off-label question — the Medical Information specialist can only respond IF the doctor asked without any prompting from a company representative. This is called an "unsolicited request." If the request is unsolicited, a truthful, balanced response can be provided. If it was somehow prompted by a company representative, responding constitutes off-label promotion — a serious regulatory violation. Every MI specialist is trained rigorously on this distinction.

Adverse Event Capture: Every time a healthcare professional or patient calls Medical Information and mentions a possible side effect — even in passing, even if they're not sure it's drug-related — the MI specialist is legally obligated to capture it as an adverse event and route it to the Pharmacovigilance team within 24 hours. This is one of the most important patient safety functions in the company.

4.4 Publication Planning — Writing the Scientific Record

Published papers in peer-reviewed journals are the currency of science. When a company's clinical trial data appears in the New England Journal of Medicine or The Lancet, it becomes part of the permanent scientific record that doctors, guideline committees, and payers rely on to make decisions.

Publication Planning is the strategic process of ensuring that the company's clinical data is published — in the right journals, at the right time, with the right authors — to maximize its scientific impact.

📌 Key Concept — Ghost Writing vs. Legitimate Medical Writing
Historically, some pharmaceutical companies paid professional writers to write papers and then had prominent physicians sign them as if they had written them — a practice called "ghost-writing." This is now strictly prohibited by industry codes (PhRMA guidelines) and journal policies (ICMJE authorship criteria). Every person listed as an author must have made a genuine intellectual contribution to the work. Medical writers are acknowledged but listed separately. Violations can result in retracted papers and regulatory consequences.

4.5 Pharmacovigilance — Keeping Patients Safe After Approval

Pharmacovigilance (PV) is the science of detecting, assessing, and preventing adverse effects of medicines after they reach the market. FDA approval does not mean a drug is perfectly safe — it means its benefits outweigh its known risks in the studied population. Post-approval, the real-world risk profile continues to emerge.

Every adverse event — every reported side effect, every unexpected reaction, every death that might be drug-related — must be collected, evaluated, and reported to the FDA. Serious and unexpected adverse events must be reported within 15 days. Less serious events go into periodic reports.

Signal Detection is the process of finding patterns in the adverse event database — a cluster of reports suggesting a new safety concern not previously identified. When a signal is detected and confirmed, it may lead to a label update, a safety communication to doctors, or in extreme cases, a market withdrawal.

REMS (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy): For drugs with serious safety risks, the FDA may require a REMS — a formal risk management program. This can include requirements for doctors to be certified before prescribing, for patients to enroll in monitoring programs, or for pharmacies to only dispense the drug under specific conditions. Medical Affairs supports REMS implementation and ongoing communication.

Publications Platform — Key Objects Reference

Publications management spans two systems: Vault Publications (Veeva) and iEnvision / Datavision (independent SaaS platforms used by many pharma Medical Affairs teams for publication planning and submission tracking). Together they provide end-to-end traceability from publication plan through journal acceptance.

📋
Publication Plan
Vault Publications · iEnvision
Annual strategic document listing all planned publications with: product, data source (trial, RWE, meta-analysis), publication type (manuscript, abstract, poster), target venue (journal or congress), planned submission date, status, and lead author. Reviewed and approved by Medical Affairs leadership at the start of each planning cycle. Drives resource allocation for medical writers and KOL authors.
📝
Manuscript / Abstract
Vault Publications · Document lifecycle
The publication document itself, versioned through the full lifecycle: Draft → Author Review → Internal Review → Submission → Revision → Acceptance. Full audit trail maintained. All author revisions tracked with timestamps. Ghost-writing is prohibited under ICMJE guidelines — medical writers are acknowledged separately. Final accepted version archived and linked to MSL reprint orders.
📬
Submission Tracker
Vault Publications · iEnvision
Tracks journal submission lifecycle: submission date, editorial decision (Accept / Revise / Reject), revision response deadline, final acceptance date, and publication DOI. Congresses tracked separately with abstract submission portal deadlines. Status feeds into the Publication Plan dashboard so Medical Affairs leadership has real-time visibility of the full publication portfolio.
👩‍🔬
Author Profile / COI
iEnvision · KOL author record
KOL author record capturing ICMJE authorship criteria met (intellectual contribution, draft review, approval, accountability), financial disclosures, and Conflict of Interest (COI) declarations. COI declarations are collected per publication and per journal — requirements differ by journal. Required by all major journals and enforced by the ICMJE Recommendations. Linked to the HCP record in Veeva Network and Veeva CRM.
📦
Reprint Order
Veeva CRM · Medical call record
Once a manuscript is accepted and published, MSLs can order reprints for unsolicited HCP dissemination. Reprint Orders are created in Veeva CRM (Medical call record type) and linked to the accepted manuscript in Vault Publications. Compliance rule: reprints may only be provided following an unsolicited request — proactive distribution to HCPs without a request constitutes promotional activity and is prohibited for MSLs. All reprint distributions are logged for Sunshine Act spend reporting.
ICMJE Authorship Standard: All listed authors must meet four criteria: (1) substantial contribution to conception or design, or data acquisition/analysis; (2) drafting or critically revising the manuscript; (3) final approval of the published version; (4) accountability for all aspects of the work. Contributors who meet only some criteria are acknowledged but not listed as authors. This rule exists specifically to prevent ghost-writing and honorary authorship.
←Ch 3 · Commercial Affairs
◈
Ch 5 · Market Access→
5
Chapter Five

Market Access — Getting the Drug to the Patient

A drug approved by the FDA is not automatically available to patients. Between regulatory approval and the moment a patient fills a prescription lies one of the most complex commercial battlegrounds in healthcare: market access. Payers — insurance companies, pharmacy benefit managers, government programmes — control the gates. Market access teams exist to negotiate those gates open.

Market access is the function responsible for ensuring that the right patients can obtain a therapy at an accessible cost, in a timely manner, through the appropriate channel. It encompasses pricing strategy, formulary negotiation, health economics evidence, reimbursement policy, government pricing compliance, and patient support programmes. A product with exceptional clinical data but poor market access may achieve only 20–30% of its commercial potential — formulary exclusions, high co-pays, and prior authorisation burdens create friction that prevents patients from starting or staying on therapy.

The Payer Landscape

Understanding who controls formulary access is the first step in market access strategy. The US payer landscape is a complex ecosystem of entities that each exert different levers on patient access and net pricing.

💊
Pharmacy Benefit Managers (PBMs)
Express Scripts · CVS Caremark · OptumRx — control ~80% of US prescription drug benefit
PBMs are the most powerful single entity in US pharmaceutical pricing. They administer prescription drug benefits for commercial health plans, self-insured employers, and government programmes. Their leverage comes from aggregating covered lives: a single PBM may manage pharmacy benefits for 100+ million people, giving them enormous negotiating power. Key PBM mechanisms: Formulary design — PBMs build and manage the formulary (the list of covered drugs and their tier placement); preferred tier placement can drive 3–5× higher script volume versus non-preferred. Rebate negotiations — manufacturers pay rebates (retrospective discounts) to secure preferred formulary placement; rebates are confidential, percentage-of-WAC, and often reach 40–70%+ for certain drug classes. Utilisation management (UM) — prior authorisation (PA) requirements, step therapy (fail-first on cheaper drugs), and quantity limits. Specialty pharmacy networks — PBMs control which specialty pharmacies can dispense high-cost biologics and specialty drugs.
🏥
Commercial Health Plans
United, Anthem, Aetna, Cigna — employer-sponsored and individual market coverage
Commercial health plans cover employer-sponsored and individual market lives. Most contract with PBMs for formulary management, but large national plans (United, Anthem) have in-house PBM capabilities. Market access teams target both the plan's Medical Director (for specialty/medical-benefit drugs administered in-office) and the formulary committee (for pharmacy-benefit drugs). Key commercial plan dynamics: Tier structure — Tier 1 (generic, lowest co-pay), Tier 2 (preferred brand), Tier 3 (non-preferred brand), Tier 4 (specialty, co-insurance); being on Tier 2 vs. Tier 3 can mean $50 vs. $100+ monthly patient cost-share. Prior authorisation criteria — plan-specific PA criteria define which patients qualify; overly restrictive PA can effectively prevent access even for on-label patients. Step therapy requirements — 30–60% of commercial plans require patients to fail generic alternatives before approving branded drugs, even in therapeutic areas where the branded drug has superior evidence.
🏛️
Medicaid
State + Federal · Best Price · AMP · Supplemental Rebates · 340B
Medicaid covers ~90 million low-income Americans across 50 state programmes. Manufacturers must participate in the Medicaid Drug Rebate Programme (MDRP) to have drugs covered by Medicaid and Medicare Part D. Best Price — manufacturers report the lowest price offered to any commercial customer (with specific exemptions); Medicaid rebate is the greater of 23.1% of AMP or the difference between AMP and Best Price. Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) — the average price manufacturers receive from wholesalers net of chargebacks and certain discounts; the basis for Medicaid rebate calculations and CMS ceiling prices. Supplemental rebates — states negotiate additional rebates above the federal floor for preferred formulary placement; preferred Medicaid formulary status can drive significant volume in states like California and New York. 340B Drug Pricing Programme — requires manufacturers to sell to covered entities (safety-net hospitals, FQHCs) at ~25% of AMP; 340B creates complex gross-to-net dynamics because these sales must be excluded from Best Price calculations.
👴
Medicare Part D & Part B
CMS · PDP sponsors · MAPD plans · ASP + 6% for Part B drugs
Medicare covers 65+ million Americans. Part D (pharmacy benefit) is delivered through private Prescription Drug Plans (PDPs) and Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plans (MAPDs). Formulary placement negotiations occur with individual Part D plan sponsors, guided by CMS formulary requirements; plans must cover at least two drugs per therapeutic class. Coverage Gap — historically known as the "donut hole"; post-IRA 2022, manufacturers provide discounts in the coverage gap. Inflation Rebate Penalty (IRA 2022) — drugs with price increases exceeding inflation (CPI-U) trigger mandatory rebates to CMS; this fundamentally constrains annual list price increases. Medicare Drug Price Negotiation (IRA 2022) — CMS can directly negotiate prices for high-expenditure drugs without generic/biosimilar competition; first negotiated prices effective 2026. Part B (medical benefit) covers physician-administered drugs (biologics, infusables); reimbursed at Average Selling Price (ASP) + 6%; buy-and-bill dynamics; hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment System rates.
🎖️
VA / DoD / Federal Supply Schedule
FAMP · Non-FAMP · Big 4 · Federal Ceiling Price
Federal government purchasers (VA, DoD, PHS, Coast Guard — collectively the "Big 4") receive the deepest statutory discounts. Federal Ceiling Price (FCP) — capped at 76% of Non-Federal Average Manufacturer Price (Non-FAMP); the Big 4 cannot be charged more than this statutory cap. Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) — VA contracts with manufacturers for FSS pricing; typically requires the most favourable price offered to comparable commercial customers. Tricare (DoD) — administers pharmacy benefits for military members and dependents; uses retail network pharmacies plus mail order and military treatment facility pharmacies. Federal pricing has significant gross-to-net implications: high volume, statutory deep discounts, but mandatory participation to retain Medicaid/Medicare programme eligibility.
🏨
IDNs, GPOs & Specialty Pharmacy
Integrated Delivery Networks · Group Purchasing Organisations · SP Hub
Integrated Delivery Networks (IDNs) — large hospital systems that negotiate directly with manufacturers for formulary inclusion across their inpatient and outpatient formularies; P&T committee approval required; IDN pharmacy directors are key market access stakeholders. Group Purchasing Organisations (GPOs) — aggregate purchasing power of multiple hospitals; GPO contracts (typically admin fees of 2–3% paid by manufacturers) are a prerequisite for hospital formulary access. Specialty Pharmacy (SP) — for high-cost specialty drugs (biologics, oncologics), manufacturers designate a limited network of specialty pharmacies authorised to dispense the product; SPs provide patient adherence support, insurance verification, and REMS compliance; SP hub services (e.g., CoverMyMeds, Lash Group) manage prior authorisation, appeals, and co-pay assistance enrolment.

Pharmaceutical Pricing Strategy

Pricing a pharmaceutical product is one of the most consequential decisions in drug commercialisation. The list price (WAC) anchors all downstream pricing calculations — yet the actual realised revenue per unit (net price) may be 40–70% below list after all discounts, rebates, and chargebacks are applied.

💰
WAC — Wholesale Acquisition Cost
List price · Public · Anchors all downstream calculations · Not what anyone actually pays
WAC is the manufacturer's published list price to wholesalers — it is set by the manufacturer and publicly available. It is the starting point for all pricing calculations. No major payer actually pays WAC; every channel receives discounts, rebates, or chargebacks below list. WAC is set strategically: high enough to accommodate the rebate concessions required for formulary access while still yielding an acceptable net price. WAC increases have historically tracked 8–10% annually, but post-IRA inflation penalties for Medicare drugs constrain future WAC increase strategy. Important distinctions: WAC is different from Average Wholesale Price (AWP) — a legacy benchmark still used in some Medicare Part B reimbursement calculations, set at WAC + ~20%.
📉
Gross-to-Net Waterfall
WAC → Gross Revenue → Net Revenue after all deductions
The gross-to-net waterfall quantifies every dollar of discount, rebate, and allowance deducted from WAC to arrive at net revenue. Typical waterfall components: Managed care rebates — largest component; paid to PBMs and health plans quarterly; recorded as a reduction to gross revenue. Medicaid statutory rebates — federal floor rebate plus state supplemental rebates. 340B discounts — mandatory discounts to covered entities; excluded from Best Price. Chargebacks — difference between contract price and WAC, paid by manufacturers to wholesalers when they sell to contract-eligible customers below WAC. Patient co-pay assistance — co-pay card redemptions reduce realised revenue. Distribution fees — fees paid to wholesalers and distributors for inventory management and data services. A typical branded drug may have gross-to-net adjustments of 40–60%; some high-rebate categories (diabetes, respiratory) exceed 70%.
🤝
Formulary Contracting & Rebate Strategy
Preferred tier · Exclusion risk · Rebate tiers · Value-based contracts
Formulary contracting is the negotiation between a manufacturer's market access team and the PBM/health plan to secure preferred tier placement in exchange for rebates. Contract structure: Base rebate — flat percentage of WAC paid for formulary inclusion (non-preferred Tier 3 access). Performance rebate — incremental rebate for preferred Tier 2 placement, conditioned on maintaining a minimum market share threshold within the formulary. Market share step-ups — additional rebate percentages triggered when the drug achieves defined market share milestones. Exclusivity rebate — paid when competitors are excluded from formulary (formulary exclusions have become common for commodity classes like statins, PPIs, and increasingly diabetes GLP-1s). Value-based contracts (VBCs) — emerging model linking rebate levels to real-world patient outcomes; e.g., manufacturer pays a higher rebate if the drug fails to achieve defined clinical outcomes in the plan's population; requires sophisticated data infrastructure.
📊
Government Pricing Compliance
Best Price · AMP · Non-FAMP · ASP · Quarterly CMS reporting
Government pricing is a mandatory quarterly reporting obligation for any manufacturer participating in the Medicaid Drug Rebate Programme (MDRP). Non-compliance carries severe penalties. Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) — average price received from wholesalers and retail pharmacies, net of chargebacks, cash discounts, and certain fees; reported quarterly to CMS; basis for Medicaid rebate, 340B ceiling price, and FAMP. Best Price (BP) — lowest price offered to any US customer in any pricing arrangement; certain transactions are excluded (340B sales, nominal pricing to charitable entities, bona fide service fees). Non-Federal Average Manufacturer Price (Non-FAMP) — basis for Federal Supply Schedule pricing and the Federal Ceiling Price calculation. Average Selling Price (ASP) — for Part B drugs; average sales price to all US purchasers net of rebates, discounts, and chargebacks; reported quarterly; used to set Medicare Part B reimbursement rate. Errors in government pricing reports can trigger retroactive rebate obligations, civil monetary penalties, and False Claims Act liability.

Health Economics & Outcomes Research (HEOR)

HEOR generates the economic and real-world evidence that underpins market access negotiations. Without a compelling economic value story, even clinically superior drugs face formulary restrictions and steep rebate demands.

📐
Cost-Effectiveness Analysis (CEA)
ICER · QALY · Cost per QALY threshold · Payer willingness-to-pay
CEA compares the cost and health outcomes of a new therapy versus the current standard of care. The primary metric is the Incremental Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ICER) — the additional cost per Quality-Adjusted Life Year (QALY) gained. ICER (non-profit) — the Institute for Clinical and Economic Review publishes independent CEA reports for high-cost drugs; ICER reports influence PBM formulary negotiations, state Medicaid supplemental rebate targets, and increasingly commercial plan coverage policies. A commonly cited US willingness-to-pay threshold is $100,000–$150,000 per QALY, though this is not a formal regulatory standard. Payer-specific models — market access teams build custom CEA models using payer-specific patient population data and local cost inputs to demonstrate value in each payer's specific context. Drugs with favourable ICER scores command higher net prices; drugs with unfavourable scores face steep rebate demands to reach cost-effectiveness thresholds.
🏦
Budget Impact Model (BIM)
Payer-specific · Covered lives · Utilisation projections · Net budget impact
While CEA answers "is this drug cost-effective for society?", the Budget Impact Model answers the question payers actually care about: "what will this drug cost MY plan if I cover it?" The BIM projects the total drug spend change for a specific health plan if it adds the new therapy to its formulary. Inputs: number of covered lives in the relevant disease population, expected market share uptake over 3 years, current standard-of-care costs, drug acquisition cost at negotiated net price, and downstream cost offsets (hospitalisations avoided, procedures avoided, productivity gains). Market access teams present customised BIMs to each payer using their specific covered-lives data. Plans with high disease prevalence in the target population may negotiate more aggressively on price than plans with low prevalence.
🌍
Real-World Evidence (RWE)
Claims data · EHR analysis · Registry studies · PRO data · Comparative effectiveness
RWE fills the evidence gaps left by randomised clinical trials — which use selected patient populations, controlled conditions, and limited follow-up periods. Sources: Claims data — insurance claims from PBMs and health plans showing real-world prescribing patterns, treatment sequences, adherence, persistence, and healthcare resource utilisation; can demonstrate that the drug reduces ER visits or hospitalisations. Electronic Health Record (EHR) data — real clinical outcomes in diverse patient populations including those excluded from trials. Patient registries — disease-specific databases capturing long-term outcomes in treated populations. Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs) — quality-of-life improvements that trial endpoints may not capture. RWE is presented to payer formulary committees to justify premium pricing or to satisfy post-approval commitments to payers who conditionally covered the drug pending real-world outcomes data.
📋
Value Dossier / AMCP Dossier
AMCP Format · Clinical + economic evidence compendium · Payer-facing
The AMCP (Academy of Managed Care Pharmacy) Format Dossier is the standard submission document manufacturers use to present clinical and economic evidence to payers. Structure: Section 1 — disease background and epidemiology; Section 2 — clinical evidence summary (all trial data, safety, quality of life); Section 3 — economic model (CEA, BIM with assumptions and references); Section 4 — product information and dosing; Section 5 — supporting references. The dossier is prepared by HEOR and medical writing teams, reviewed by regulatory to ensure off-label content compliance, and submitted to payer pharmacy and therapeutics (P&T) committees as part of the formulary review process. A well-constructed dossier can accelerate formulary coverage decisions from 12+ months to 3–6 months.

Patient Access Programmes

Even with formulary placement, high patient cost-share can prevent patients from starting or staying on therapy. Patient access programmes address this gap and are a critical lever in total access strategy.

💳
Co-pay Assistance Cards
Commercially insured patients only · OOP reduction · Accumulators · Maximisers
Co-pay assistance cards (also called co-pay cards or coupons) allow commercially insured patients to reduce their out-of-pocket cost-share. Manufacturer funds the difference between the patient's plan cost-share and a set maximum patient co-pay (e.g., patient pays $10/month, manufacturer pays remaining $90 to the pharmacy). Restrictions: Federally insured patients excluded — co-pay cards cannot be used by Medicare, Medicaid, or other government-insured patients (Anti-Kickback Statute violation); patients must attest to commercial insurance status. Accumulator adjustment programmes — PBMs have implemented accumulators that prevent co-pay card payments from counting toward a patient's deductible/out-of-pocket maximum; once the card maximum is exhausted, patients face full cost-share. Maximiser programmes — programmes designed to capture the full co-pay card benefit (up to $10,000+/year) while keeping the patient's cost-share near zero; manufacturers have responded with maximiser-specific card designs.
🆓
Patient Assistance Programmes (PAPs)
Uninsured / underinsured · Income-based · Free drug · Foundation support
PAPs provide free or deeply discounted drug to uninsured or underinsured patients who meet income eligibility criteria (typically ≤400% of the federal poverty level). Manufacturers are not required to offer PAPs but virtually all branded manufacturers do as part of their access commitment. PAP mechanics: patient submits income documentation to the manufacturer or a third-party hub; manufacturer ships free drug directly to patient or provides vouchers; some manufacturers instead direct patients to charitable foundations (PAN Foundation, HealthWell Foundation) which provide grants for premium assistance and cost-sharing. Medicare patients ineligible for co-pay cards may qualify for foundation grants. PAP programmes are closely scrutinised by OIG for compliance with Anti-Kickback Statute safe harbours; all PAP decisions must be made without reference to the patient's prescribing volume.
🔓
Prior Authorisation & Appeals Support
PA criteria navigation · Peer-to-peer review · Appeals management · Bridge supply
Prior authorisation (PA) is a payer requirement that physicians obtain plan approval before prescribing a drug. PA denial is one of the most common barriers to therapy initiation. Hub services support PA navigation: Benefit investigation — determines the patient's plan, formulary tier, and PA requirements before the prescription is submitted. PA initiation — hub staff complete and submit PA forms on behalf of the prescriber. Peer-to-peer facilitation — when initial PA is denied, the hub connects the prescriber with the plan's Medical Director for a clinical peer-to-peer review call. Appeals management — for continued denials, hub staff manage the formal appeals process including administrative appeals and external independent reviews. Bridge supply — free starter samples or emergency supply to ensure patients do not go without therapy during PA processing delays (which average 3–17 business days depending on drug and plan).
📈
Access Analytics & Market Intelligence
Formulary tracking · PA denial rates · NRx/TRx pull-through · Net price realisation
Market access analytics tracks the real-world impact of coverage decisions on prescription volume and revenue. Key metrics: Formulary coverage — percentage of covered commercial, Medicare Part D, and Medicaid lives with unrestricted or preferred access; tracked weekly by formulary intelligence vendors (Managed Markets Insight & Technology, Fingertip Formulary). PA denial rate & appeal success rate — early warning indicator of access barriers; rising denial rates may require PA criteria renegotiation with payers. Pull-through rate — percentage of written prescriptions that are actually dispensed (abandoned prescriptions indicate access barriers). Net price realisation — actual net revenue per unit versus plan; variance analysis identifies whether realised pricing aligns with contracting assumptions. Gross-to-net reconciliation — quarterly process to true up actual rebate payments against accruals; managed through Model N and financial planning systems.

Model N — Revenue Management & Government Pricing Platform

Model N is the leading enterprise software platform for pharmaceutical revenue management. It automates the complex, compliance-critical processes of government price reporting, contract management, rebate administration, and chargebacks — processes that, if done manually or incorrectly, create enormous financial exposure and False Claims Act liability.

🏛️
Model N — Government Pricing (GP) Module
AMP · Best Price · Non-FAMP · ASP · Medicaid rebate invoicing · CMS reporting
The Government Pricing module is Model N's core differentiator. It automates the quarterly calculation and CMS reporting of AMP, Best Price, Non-FAMP, and ASP — calculations that involve processing millions of transaction records across every channel, applying complex statutory exclusions, and producing audit-ready reports. Transaction ingestion — Model N ingests sales data from wholesalers (via 867 data feeds), chargebacks, contract sales (via 844/845 EDI), and direct sales; each transaction is classified by channel, customer type, and applicable pricing programme. AMP calculation engine — applies 42 CFR Part 447 regulatory rules to compute weighted-average AMP by NDC by quarter; handles complex scenarios including restatements, late submissions, and newly approved products. Best Price determination — scans all transaction data and pricing arrangements to identify the lowest price in any configuration; flags potential Best Price violations before submission. CMS submission — generates the MDRP quarterly submission in the required XML format for the CMS Drug Data Reporting for Medicaid (DDR) system; maintains submission audit trail. Medicaid rebate invoicing — processes Medicaid rebate invoices from all 50 state Medicaid agencies; validates invoice amounts against CMS-calculated rebate obligations; manages disputes and supplemental rebate payments.
📑
Model N — Contract Management
Commercial contracts · Managed care · GPO · Government · Chargeback processing
Model N manages the full lifecycle of all pricing contracts — from creation and approval through execution, chargeback processing, and expiration. Contract authoring — contracts are built in Model N's contract management module with pricing tiers, eligibility criteria (customer type, GPO membership), effective dates, and performance conditions; approved through a workflow and published to the contract repository. Eligibility determination — when a wholesaler submits a chargeback claim (asserting that a customer purchased at a sub-WAC contract price), Model N validates: is the customer eligible for the claimed contract? Was the purchase date within the contract period? Does the claim match the wholesaler's sales data? Chargeback processing — validated chargebacks are approved and the differential between contract price and WAC is credited to the wholesaler; invalid chargebacks are disputed with supporting data. Contract performance tracking — Model N tracks market share and volume performance against contract thresholds to calculate earned versus unearned rebate tiers; ensures rebate payments are paid only when performance conditions are met.
💸
Model N — Rebate Management
Managed care rebates · Medicaid supplementals · State contract rebates · Accruals
Rebate management in Model N tracks every rebate obligation across commercial managed care, Medicaid supplemental, state contracts, and federal pricing programmes. Rebate accruals — for financial reporting (GAAP), manufacturers must accrue rebate liabilities in the same period as the associated revenue; Model N calculates accruals based on contracted rates, utilisation data from PBMs and plans, and government price calculations; feeds the P&L as a gross-to-net revenue reduction. Managed care rebate settlement — PBMs and health plans submit quarterly utilisation data; Model N calculates the rebate due, generates payment instructions, and reconciles against prior accruals; performance rebate tiers are evaluated based on submitted utilisation versus contract thresholds. Medicaid supplemental rebates — state Medicaid programmes negotiate supplemental rebates above the federal floor; Model N tracks each state's supplemental agreement, processes state-submitted utilisation, and calculates state-specific supplemental payments. Dispute management — when payers submit incorrect utilisation data or calculate rebates erroneously, Model N generates dispute letters with supporting data and tracks dispute resolution timelines.
🔗
Model N — 340B Programme Management
Ceiling price · Covered entity eligibility · Duplicate discount prevention · HRSA compliance
The 340B Drug Pricing Programme requires manufacturers to sell to eligible covered entities (safety-net hospitals, Federally Qualified Health Centres, Ryan White HIV/AIDS clinics, etc.) at a ceiling price of approximately 23.1% of AMP — the same discount as the Medicaid federal floor rebate. Model N manages 340B compliance: Covered entity eligibility verification — Model N integrates with the HRSA 340B Office of Pharmacy Affairs (OPA) database to verify which entities are eligible for 340B pricing; purchases by ineligible entities at 340B prices create contract pharmacy and replenishment reconciliation errors. Ceiling price calculation — quarterly 340B ceiling prices are calculated from AMP and published in HRSA's 340B ceiling price database; Model N automates this calculation and ensures pricing systems are updated before the next quarter. Duplicate discount prevention — federal law prohibits 340B discounts and Medicaid rebates from being applied to the same dispensing event; Model N flags and manages these "duplicate discount" scenarios through data matching and carve-out programme administration. Contract pharmacy monitoring — as 340B has expanded to contract pharmacy arrangements (340B-eligible entities dispensing through retail pharmacy chains), Model N tracks these arrangements and manages manufacturer restrictions on contract pharmacy participation.
🔄
Model N — Gross-to-Net Analytics
Net revenue realisation · Waterfall reporting · Variance analysis · Forecast vs. actual
Model N's analytics layer transforms transactional contract, rebate, and government pricing data into the gross-to-net waterfall reporting that finance and market access leadership use to manage the business. Net revenue by channel — breaks down actual net revenue realisation by payer segment (commercial managed care, Medicare Part D, Medicaid, cash/uninsured, 340B, federal); reveals which channels are most and least profitable after all contractual deductions. Rebate rate analysis — actual rebate rates paid versus contracted rates; identifies whether portfolio companies are receiving the formulary positions they contracted for and paying rebates correctly. Accrual-to-actual reconciliation — compares financial accruals recorded in the G/L against actual payments made; large variances signal accrual methodology problems or unexpected payer behaviour. IRA inflation penalty projection — as of 2023, drugs with Medicare price increases above CPI must pay inflation rebates to CMS; Model N calculates projected penalties under different pricing scenarios to inform WAC increase decisions. All analytics are exportable to Anaplan for integrated financial and commercial planning.
⚖️
Model N — Compliance & Audit Trail
21 CFR Part 11 · False Claims Act defence · Audit-ready documentation · SOX controls
Government pricing errors are not merely accounting mistakes — they are potential False Claims Act violations that can result in treble damages and exclusion from federal health programmes. Model N is built as an audit-ready, compliance-first platform: Complete audit trail — every calculation, every data input, every user action, every submission is logged with user ID, timestamp, and before/after values; creates the documentation chain needed to defend government pricing calculations in CMS audits or DOJ investigations. Restatement management — when historical AMP or Best Price calculations need correction (due to late-arriving data or calculation errors), Model N manages the restatement process, recalculates affected quarters, and submits amendments to CMS with full change documentation. SOX controls — as a system that directly impacts publicly reported financial statements (through gross-to-net accruals), Model N is subject to Sarbanes-Oxley internal controls; access controls, segregation of duties, and approval workflows are configured to satisfy SOX requirements. Pricing policy enforcement — pricing rules are configured in the system once, approved through governance, and automatically applied to every transaction; eliminates manual calculation errors that create compliance exposure.

Anaplan — Connected Planning for Market Access

While Model N manages the transactional reality of contracts and government pricing, Anaplan provides the planning, forecasting, and scenario modelling layer — connecting market access strategy with financial targets, commercial planning, and supply chain. Anaplan's in-memory calculation engine and connected planning architecture allow market access teams to model complex "what-if" scenarios across WAC pricing, rebate strategies, formulary assumptions, and patient access programme investments in real time.

📊
Anaplan — Gross-to-Net Planning & Forecasting
Rebate accrual forecast · Net revenue planning · GTN ratio modelling · Sensitivity analysis
The gross-to-net planning model in Anaplan is the central forecasting tool for market access finance. It connects formulary coverage assumptions, contracted rebate rates, government pricing projections, and patient volume forecasts into a unified net revenue model. Connected data inputs — Anaplan pulls actual gross sales from ERP (SAP/Oracle), actual rebate payments from Model N, formulary coverage from market intelligence vendors, and NRx/TRx demand data from IQVIA; all inputs refresh on defined schedules so the GTN model is always based on current actuals. Rolling forecast — Anaplan's continuous planning architecture supports a rolling 18-month forecast that updates automatically as actuals are posted; eliminates the quarterly "black box" forecasting cycle of spreadsheet-based planning. Scenario modelling — market access teams model formulary coverage scenarios (e.g., what happens to net revenue if a major PBM excludes the drug in Q3?) with instant recalculation across all connected metrics including financial plan, sales quota, and supply demand signals. GTN ratio trending — tracks gross-to-net ratio over time and flags deterioration (e.g., rising rebate rates due to competitive pressure) as an early warning to pricing strategy teams.
🎯
Anaplan — Contracting Strategy & Rebate Optimisation
Rebate modelling · Tier placement optimisation · Portfolio strategy · Deal economics
Before entering a formulary negotiation, market access teams use Anaplan to model the economics of every deal scenario. Rebate-volume trade-off modelling — the core question is: at what rebate rate does preferred tier placement generate enough incremental prescription volume to offset the rebate cost and produce higher net revenue than a lower-rebate non-preferred position? Anaplan models this trade-off using demand elasticity curves calibrated to historical access data. Portfolio contracting — for companies with multiple products, Anaplan models portfolio bundle contracts where a preferred position for Product A is traded against a preferred position for Product B; cross-portfolio deal optimisation requires simultaneous modelling of all product-payer combinations. Exclusivity economics — models the incremental volume (and associated incremental rebate cost) of paying an exclusivity premium versus the benefit of a formulary exclusively. Contract performance simulation — for performance-tiered contracts, Anaplan simulates the probability of hitting each tier threshold based on historical market share data and forecasted demand, allowing teams to negotiate tiers that are achievable but stretch commercial performance.
🌐
Anaplan — Brand Planning & LOE Forecasting
Launch forecast · Loss of exclusivity · Patient flow modelling · Lifecycle management
Anaplan connects market access modelling to the broader brand planning and lifecycle management process. Launch revenue forecast — integrates formulary coverage ramp assumptions (what % of covered lives will have preferred access in months 1, 6, 12 post-launch) with TRx demand forecasts and GTN assumptions to project net revenue by quarter for the first 3–5 years. Loss of Exclusivity (LOE) planning — models the revenue cliff when patent protection expires and generics enter: generic penetration curves, managed care formulary switching, authorised generic strategy, and the residual branded volume that remains. Patient journey modelling — builds a patient flow model from diagnosis to therapy initiation, tracking conversion rates at each step (diagnosed → treated → switched to this drug) and the access barriers (PA denial rate, co-pay burden, step therapy) that reduce conversion; used to size patient support programme investment. Indication expansion — models revenue impact of a new indication submission including incremental formulary negotiations, label expansion timing, and incremental patient population size.
🔗
Anaplan — Connected Planning Architecture
Finance · Supply chain · Commercial · HR · Single source of truth · API integrations
Anaplan's differentiated architecture is its ability to connect plans across functions in a single in-memory platform, eliminating the "Excel island" problem where each function maintains its own disconnected version of the forecast. Finance integration — Anaplan's net revenue model feeds directly into the financial P&L forecast in the corporate financial planning model; when market access updates a contracting assumption, the impact on gross profit flows immediately to finance. Supply chain integration — demand forecasts from Anaplan drive supply planning (manufacturing batch sizing, distribution inventory levels); access-driven demand shocks (new formulary win adding 5M covered lives) are immediately visible to supply planners. Commercial quota setting — net revenue forecasts by territory and product flow from Anaplan to the commercial team's quota-setting model; quotas reflect realistic net-price expectations, not inflated WAC-based numbers. API ecosystem — Anaplan connects to Model N (for actual GTN data), SAP/Oracle (for financial actuals), IQVIA/Symphony (for market data), and Veeva CRM (for commercial activity and forecast data) through a pre-built connector library and CloudWorks integration platform. The result: a single, continuously updated enterprise forecast that replaces the quarterly 6-week budgeting cycle.
💡 Model N + Anaplan: How They Work Together
Model N is the system of record for what actually happened — the transactional truth of every contract, chargeback, rebate payment, and government price calculation. Anaplan is the system of intelligence for what will happen — the forward-looking planning, scenario modelling, and cross-functional alignment engine. Data flows from Model N into Anaplan quarterly (or more frequently via API) to ground planning assumptions in transactional reality. A contracting team member negotiating a new PBM agreement in Anaplan's deal modelling tool is working with actual current-quarter rebate rates pulled from Model N, actual formulary coverage data from market intelligence feeds, and actual demand data from IQVIA — producing a deal model that is both analytically rigorous and operationally grounded. The two systems together close the loop between strategy and execution in market access.
←Ch 4 · Medical Affairs
◈
Ch 6 · The Firewall→
6
Chapter Six

The Firewall — How Commercial and Medical Work Together Without Crossing the Line

Two powerful teams. One product. One set of patients. But very different mandates — and a legally mandated separation designed to protect the integrity of science. This chapter explains one of the most nuanced organizational design challenges in any industry.

🏛️ Think of it this way — The Church and State Analogy
In many democratic societies, there is a principle of separation between church (religious authority) and state (civil authority). Each has its own domain. Each respects the other's independence. And when they blur, problems arise for everyone. Medical Affairs is the "church" — it holds scientific authority. Commercial Affairs is the "state" — it holds commercial authority. The firewall is the constitutional separation between them. When it is violated, patients, doctors, and ultimately the company suffer the consequences.

What the Firewall Prohibits

  • MSLs cannot receive their call plans from the Sales team or be directed to visit specific HCPs for commercial reasons
  • Commercial teams cannot use individual MSL meeting insights to direct sales targeting
  • Medical Affairs cannot attend or observe promotional sales calls
  • Promotional materials cannot be presented by MSLs to HCPs
  • Sales representatives cannot suggest that a patient ask their doctor about off-label uses

What the Two Teams Share (Compliantly)

Despite the firewall, Commercial and Medical Affairs must collaborate. They do so through structured, documented processes:

The Brand Team: Both teams sit on the cross-functional brand team. They share market research outputs, competitive intelligence summaries, and high-level patient data — always in aggregate form, never individual HCP-level commercial data flowing to MSLs.

Medical-Commercial Interface (MCI) Meetings: Quarterly formal meetings where Medical Affairs shares aggregate scientific insights (what KOLs are asking, what evidence gaps exist) and Commercial shares market dynamics (what access barriers exist, what HCPs are saying through sales channels). Every meeting is documented to demonstrate that the firewall is maintained.

Speaker Bureau Training: KOLs who participate in commercial speaker programs (promotional events organized by Sales) must be trained on approved promotional content. Medical Affairs delivers this training to ensure scientific accuracy — even though the bureau serves commercial purposes.

Field Question Referrals: When a sales representative hears a medical question from a doctor that is outside their scope, they refer the question to the Medical Information line or to the regional MSL. This is the compliant handshake between the two teams.

"The firewall does not make Commercial and Medical enemies. It makes their cooperation honest. When a KOL trusts an MSL because they know the MSL is independent of Sales, that trust is the most valuable currency a pharmaceutical company owns."
←Ch 5 · Market Access
◈
Ch 7 · R&D Bridge→
7
Chapter Seven

The R&D Bridge — How a Scientific Discovery Becomes a Commercial Product

The hand-off from R&D to Commercial and Medical Affairs is not a single moment — it is a years-long, carefully orchestrated transition that starts the moment Phase 2b data looks promising and doesn't end until years after the product is on the market.

Stage 1 — Phase 2b: First Introduction (T-48 to T-36 Months)

When a drug enters Phase 2b, Commercial and Medical Affairs are formally introduced. The Commercial Assessment process begins: how large is the market? Who are the patients? What will payers demand in terms of evidence? What commercial profile (what label) would be most valuable?

Commercial provides market intelligence feedback: what label claims would doctors find most compelling? What comparator would make the strongest competitive statement? Medical Affairs identifies the scientific questions that KOLs will ask and begins building the evidence gap analysis.

Stage 2 — Phase 3 Design: Influencing the Science (T-36 to T-18 Months)

Before the Phase 3 protocol is locked, Commercial and Medical Affairs have one critical window to shape the study. They provide input on:

  • The comparator drug — which competitor should the drug be tested against? Commercial provides landscape intelligence; Medical Affairs advises on scientific validity.
  • Patient-reported outcomes — does the trial capture quality-of-life measures that payers and guideline committees will value? Medical Affairs brings KOL perspective; Commercial brings payer perspective.
  • Real-world evidence studies — what parallel RWE studies should run alongside the pivotal trial? Medical Affairs designs these.

Once the protocol is locked, nobody can change these parameters. This window is precious — missing it means living with the consequences for a decade.

Stage 3 — NDA Filing: Labeling Strategy (T-18 to T-6 Months)

As the NDA is prepared, all three functions join the Labeling Strategy Team. The product label — every word of the prescribing information — determines what can be said commercially. Broader efficacy claims mean more patients are eligible. A cleaner safety section means fewer contraindications. Every word is negotiated with the FDA over months.

Medical Affairs validates the scientific basis for each proposed claim. Commercial argues for the commercial value of each claim. Regulatory Affairs manages the actual FDA negotiation. The label that emerges from this process determines the commercial destiny of the product.

Stage 4 — Post-Approval: The Intelligence Loop (T+0 Onward)

After approval, the intelligence flows in both directions. Medical Affairs feeds RWE data and KOL clinical insights back to R&D for the next phase of lifecycle management (new indications, new patient populations, new dosing regimens). Commercial feeds market performance data and payer feedback to R&D to prioritize which lifecycle extensions are most valuable. R&D uses this combined intelligence to plan the next development program. The loop is continuous.

💡 Lifecycle Management (LCM) — The Next Chapter
A drug rarely stays the same throughout its commercial life. Over time, Medical Affairs identifies new patient populations who could benefit. R&D runs additional trials. The company files supplemental NDAs for new indications. A drug approved for epilepsy might later be approved for bipolar disorder. A drug approved for adults might later be approved for children. Each new approval extends the product's commercial life and justifies continued R&D investment — this is called Lifecycle Management, and both Commercial and Medical Affairs are deeply involved in shaping it.
←Ch 6 · The Firewall
◈
Ch 8 · The Launch→
8
Chapter Eight

The Big Launch! — From Laboratory to Pharmacy

A pharmaceutical product launch is unlike anything else in business. No Super Bowl campaign, no iPhone unveiling, no retail store opening comes close to the complexity, the stakes, and the sheer coordinated human effort required to bring a new medicine to the world. This chapter walks through every phase.

🚀 Think of it this way — The Space Shuttle Launch Analogy
Launching a pharmaceutical product is like launching a space shuttle. Scientists spend years building the rocket (the drug and its clinical evidence). Engineers spend months building all the ground systems (commercial infrastructure, IT systems, supply chain). Thousands of people coordinate in perfect sequence. And when the moment comes, there is no going back — you either launch cleanly or you have a very public failure. A pharmaceutical launch that goes wrong can cost hundreds of millions of dollars and set a company back for years.

The Launch Clock — 36 Months to Go

Launch preparation begins approximately 36 months before the anticipated FDA approval date — known as the PDUFA date. Everything that must be done is mapped against this clock. Here is the full journey:

📐

Phase 1 — Strategic Foundation (T-36 to T-24 Months)

"What is the commercial opportunity, and how do we organize to capture it?"

At T-36 months, Commercial Affairs begins the Commercial Opportunity Assessment. Using Phase 3 interim data and the anticipated label, they model: what is the realistic revenue potential of this drug? How large is the patient population? How competitive is the market? What share can we realistically capture?

These financial models drive the most important structural decisions: How large should the sales force be? Do we need a specialty sales force (for academic medical centres) in addition to a primary care force? Should we hire directly or partner with a contract sales organization?

Medical Affairs, in parallel, finalizes its Medical Plan. Which evidence gaps must be filled before launch? Which Phase 3b studies need to start immediately? Which Key Opinion Leaders need to be engaged to build scientific credibility before approval?

Both teams begin building their organizational structure. Senior leaders are hired first — VP Medical Affairs, VP Sales, VP Market Access. They bring their directors. Directors hire managers. This organizational build takes 18–24 months for a major launch.

🏗️

Phase 2 — Pre-Launch Build (T-24 to T-6 Months)

"Build every system, hire every person, create every tool — ready before approval."

This is the most intensive phase. Here is what happens across the organization:

Commercial: Disease Awareness (T-24 to T-0 Months)

Before FDA approval, the company cannot promote the drug — it has no approved label yet. But it can invest in disease awareness campaigns — educating doctors and patients about the condition the drug will treat, without mentioning the unapproved drug. This primes the market. When doctors learn about the disease burden and unmet needs before launch, they are more receptive to information about a new treatment when approval comes.

Commercial: Sales Force Hiring and Training (T-9 to T-3 Months)

The sales force — sometimes 50, sometimes 500 people, depending on the drug — is hired approximately six months before launch. They go through an intensive 6–8 week training program covering: the disease biology, the clinical trial data, the competitive landscape, the approved promotional messages, the compliance rules, and the selling skills specific to the therapeutic area.
